title: Exposure Control and ALARA Review
narrationScript: At Level One we handed you three levers. Time, distance, and shielding. At Level Two the levers are the same, but the job is different, because now you are the one who plans the shot, briefs the crew, and answers for every dosimeter on the job. This lesson is a review with the rank turned up. We are not going to redefine the inverse square law. We are going to use it the way a working Level Two uses it, before the source ever leaves the device. Start where every plan starts, with the source itself. Activity decays, so the number on the certificate is history. Suppose the paperwork says one hundred curies of iridium one ninety-two, certified seventy-four days ago. Seventy-four days is one half-life, so today you are holding fifty curies. Iridium gives roughly half a roentgen per hour per curie at one meter, so the open source produces about twenty-five roentgen per hour at one meter. Every other number in the plan grows from that one. Now place your boundary. Typical practice posts the restricted area where the rate falls to about two millirem per hour. Run the inverse square law and the answer is sobering. Twenty-five thousand millirem per hour at one meter does not fall to two until you are out around one hundred ten meters. That is a circle more than two football fields across, in a yard full of fitters and crane operators. This is why a Level Two treats the collimator as part of the plan, not an accessory. A collimator typically cuts the rate by a factor of ten or more in the directions it shields, and aiming the primary beam into the ground or a masonry wall handles the direction it cannot. A factor of ten in rate pulls the boundary in by the square root of ten, about three point two, so one hundred ten meters becomes roughly thirty-five. Then the meter, not the math, sets the rope, because scatter always redraws the circle a little. Your crank position gets the same arithmetic. At fifteen meters of cable the open rate would be about one hundred ten millirem per hour. Collimated, call it eleven. A two-minute crank-out, exposure, and crank-in costs about four tenths of a millirem. Twenty welds in a shift, seven or eight millirem. Now you have something better than a feeling. You have a budget. Time control at Level Two is exactly that, a budget. Estimate the dose for the shift before work starts, then read the pocket dosimeters against it. When the actual matches the plan, your habits are working. When somebody comes back at three or four times the estimate, that is not a number to shrug at because it is under the limit. That is a finding. Somebody is standing near the guide tube during crank-out, or waiting inside the boundary instead of behind the wall, and you find the habit and fix it the same day. That is what evaluating work practices against ALARA actually means. ALARA says doses must be as low as reasonably achievable, and the key word is reasonably. It does not demand zero. It demands that you never accept dose that planning, equipment, or scheduling could remove at reasonable cost. The five rem per year occupational limit is a ceiling, never an allowance. So audit your own jobs the way a program reviewer would. Was every shot collimated that could be. Was the crank at full cable length with structure in between. Were the high-dose shots scheduled for an empty shop. Was the boundary surveyed, posted, and watched the whole time the source was out. Did everyone read and record a dosimeter every day, and did anyone ask why when a number looked odd. A good review also normalizes the numbers. Millirem per weld is a better measure of practice than millirem per month, because workload varies and habits hide inside totals. And remember who else you are protecting. Members of the public and unbadged plant workers are typically held to far lower limits than radiographers, about one hundred millirem in a year and about two millirem in any single hour. That is why the two millirem per hour line exists, and why it is watched, not just posted. Controlling personnel exposure means everyone's exposure, not just yours. Plan the shot with numbers, defend the boundary, budget the millirem, and question every reading that does not match the plan. Do that for a career and your dose record stays what it should be. Boring, low, and explainable, on purpose.
imagePrompt: A senior radiographer marks a boundary plan on a clipboard beside a gamma projector, coiled drive cable, and collimator in a crowded steel fabrication yard while a trainee posts radiation signs on rope stanchions in the background.

sections.heading: Level II Means You Own the Plan
sections.body:
At Level I you learned the three levers - time, distance, shielding. At Level II the levers have not changed, but your relationship to them has: you write the plan that everyone else's dose depends on, you brief it, and you answer for every dosimeter on the job. **Controlling personnel exposure** is now a planning task that finishes before the device is ever unlocked.

The plan starts with the source, because activity is a moving target. The certificate says **100 Ci of Ir-192 - certified 74 days ago**. That is one half-life, so today you hold **50 Ci**, and at roughly **0.5 R/h per curie at 1 meter**, the open source produces about **25 R/h at 1 m**. Every other number in the plan grows from that one: the boundary radius, the crank-position rate, the dose budget for the shift.

And widen the definition of *personnel*. The plan protects your assistant and trainee, but also the fitters, crane operators, and visitors who never signed up for occupational dose - people typical regulations hold to about **100 mrem per year and about 2 mrem in any one hour**. That is why the restricted-area rope lives near the measured **2 mR/h** line, and why it is watched, not merely posted.

sections.heading: Run the Numbers Before the Source Moves
sections.body:
Work the geometry before rigging, while changes are still free.

- **Open-beam boundary.** 25 R/h at 1 m is 25,000 mR/h. The 2 mR/h line lands at d = √(25,000 ÷ 2) ≈ **110 m** - a circle wider than two football fields, in a yard full of trades. The calculation just told you, before the source moved, that this job is not shot open-beam.
- **Collimated boundary.** A collimator typically cuts the rate by a **factor of 10 or more** in the directions it shields; aim the primary beam into masonry or the ground to handle the direction it cannot. Rate ÷ 10 pulls the boundary in by √10 ≈ 3.2, so 110 m becomes roughly **35 m**. Workable - and the survey meter, not the arithmetic, sets the final rope, because scatter off slabs, steel, and the part redraws every calculated circle.
- **Crank position.** At 15 m of cable the open rate would be about 110 mR/h; collimated, call it **11 mR/h**. A two-minute crank-out, exposure, and crank-in costs about **0.4 mR per shot** - and now you can budget a shift instead of guessing at one.

This is what time, distance, and shielding mean at Level II: not three slogans but one connected calculation - decay, output, inverse square, collimation - run before anyone touches a crank.

sections.caption: Dose map of a planned collimated shot: crank position, beam direction, and the surveyed 2 mR/h boundary, with the open-beam alternative shown for contrast.

sections.heading: Time Is a Budget, Not a Stopwatch
sections.body:
Level I taught that dose = rate × time. Level II turns it into a **pre-job dose budget**: estimate the dose for each task, multiply by the shot count, and write the number down before work starts. The table below budgets the header job at about **8 mR for the shift**. Now the pocket dosimeters have something to be compared against. When readings match the budget, your habits are working; when one comes back at three or four times the estimate, that is a **finding**, not a footnote - someone is waiting near the guide tube during crank-out, or staging film inside the falling field, and you find the habit and fix it the same day.

Time control itself still looks like Level I done seriously: dry-run the moves while the source is locked, stage film, markers, and the next setup outside the boundary, crank smoothly - a fumbled connector or a re-shot weld doubles time near the source - and place the trainee where waiting is free. The budget simply makes the discipline visible and auditable.

sections.caption: Pre-job dose budget for a 20-weld shift - 50 Ci Ir-192, collimated, crank at 15 m.
rows: Film change at the guide tube (source surveyed shielded)
Same crank work, open beam (for contrast)

sections.title: The boundary is yours to defend
sections.body: Post the restricted-area rope where the **survey meter reads about 2 mR/h** - never where habit or the calculator says - and keep the line under **continuous surveillance** the whole time the source is out of the shield. Nobody crosses, including supervision. Re-survey after **any** change of source, collimation, or geometry. The people behind that rope are typically limited to about **2 mrem in any one hour**; the rope is how you keep a promise they never knew you made.

sections.heading: Evaluating Work Practices Against ALARA
sections.body:
**ALARA - as low as reasonably achievable** - is not a feeling of carefulness; it is an audit standard. The occupational limit of **5 rem per year** is a ceiling, never an allowance, and ALARA asks one question of every practice: *is this dose avoidable at reasonable cost?* If yes, avoiding it is the requirement.

Evaluate a job the way a program reviewer would:

- **Collimation** - was every shot collimated that could be, or do open beams happen for convenience?
- **Distance and structure** - cranks at full cable length with walls or vessels in between, chosen at the dry run?
- **Scheduling** - high-dose work pushed to empty-shop hours?
- **Boundaries** - set by survey, watched continuously, re-surveyed when anything changed?
- **Dosimetry discipline** - pocket dosimeters zeroed, read, and recorded daily, with unusual readings investigated the day they appear?

Good programs add **investigation levels** well below the limits, so review triggers long before anything legal is at stake, and they trend a normalized metric - **mR per weld** tells you about practice, while mR per month mostly tells you about workload. The goal of all of it is a dose history that looks boring: low, flat, and explainable, on purpose.

sections.caption: The same twenty welds run two ways: open beam versus collimated, with the crank-position rate, per-shot dose, shift dose, and boundary radius compared.

figures.title: Dose map of a planned gamma shot
Same welds, two plans: open beam versus collimated

interactive.title: Plan the Shot: Level II ALARA Decision Trainer
interactive.intro: Day shift in a congested fabrication yard: 14 girth welds on a header with an Ir-192 source certified at 100 Ci, 74 days ago - so about 50 Ci and 25 R/h at 1 m today. Fitters and a crane crew are working nearby. You are the Level II running the job, with one trainee.

steps.prompt: First planning step: the open-beam boundary. Where does the inverse square law put the 2 mR/h line if you shoot without a collimator?
options: About 35 m - close enough to rope off and proceed
About 110 m - far too large for this yard, so the plan must change
About 12 m - the yard fence already covers it
steps.feedback: 25 R/h at 1 m is 25,000 mR/h, and 2 mR/h is not reached until √(25,000 ÷ 2) ≈ 110 m. A boundary that size is unworkable here - which is the point: the calculation tells you NOW, before the source moves, that this job gets a collimator and a beam direction into masonry or ground.

steps.prompt: With the collimator installed (a factor of ten in its shielded directions) you estimate the boundary near 35 m. The fitter foreman asks to keep his crew working 40 m out, 'behind the crane.' Your call?
options: Agree - 40 m is beyond the 35 m estimate
Agree, but only if the crew promises to stay behind the crane mats
Survey with the collimator installed, post the rope where the meter actually reads 2 mR/h, and keep the line under continuous surveillance
steps.feedback: The estimate plans the job; the meter sets the rope. Scatter off slabs, steel, and the part redraws every calculated circle, and a crane is not a surveyed shield. Post where the instrument reads 2 mR/h, watch the line the whole time the source is out, and nobody crosses - that is how you control the exposure of people who never signed up for any.

steps.prompt: Your trainee proposes cutting the source-to-film distance in half 'so the exposures run four times faster and we all get less dose.' Evaluate the idea.
options: Good ALARA thinking - less exposure time means less dose to the crew
Wrong on both counts - the crew waits outside the boundary during exposures anyway, and halving the distance doubles geometric unsharpness, risking re-shots that add real dose
Acceptable, provided the boundary distance is doubled to compensate
steps.feedback: Personnel dose accumulates during crank-out, approaches, film changes, and boundary work - not while everyone waits at distance during the exposure clock. Meanwhile Ug = F·t/d grows as d shrinks, and a failed unsharpness or sensitivity check means re-shooting the weld: more handling, more dose. ALARA evaluates real dose pathways, not intuitions.

steps.prompt: At lunch your pocket dosimeter reads 4 mR; the trainee's reads 26 mR for the same seven shots. Both are far below any limit. What does ALARA require?
options: Nothing - both readings are legal
Investigate today: watch where the trainee stands during crank-out and film staging, find the habit, correct it, and record what you found
Swap dosimeters to rule out a defective instrument, and otherwise carry on
steps.feedback: A six-fold spread on identical work is a finding, not a footnote. The usual culprits: waiting near the guide tube during crank-out, or staging film inside the falling field. Checking the instrument is fair, but it accompanies the investigation - ALARA treats every unexplained millirem as a question that needs an answer the same day.

steps.prompt: Last weld of the day: rigging only allows 7 m of drive cable instead of your planned 15 m. At 7 m the collimated rate is about 50 mR/h instead of 11. It is one two-minute shot. Decide.
options: Take the shot - under 2 mR of extra dose, once, is trivial
Have the trainee crank this one so the dose spreads across the crew
Re-rig the layout: move the device, reroute the cable, and restore full distance plus structure
steps.feedback: Re-rig. The dose is small, but it is avoidable at reasonable cost, and avoidable dose accepted for convenience is exactly what ALARA forbids - and what dose-trend reviews eventually find. Spreading the dose to the trainee fails both ALARA and leadership.

checkQuestions.question: Your Ir-192 source was certified at 80 Ci, 148 days ago. Using 0.5 R/h per curie at 1 m, the open-source output today is about:
options: 20 R/h at 1 m
10 R/h at 1 m
5 R/h at 1 m
40 R/h at 1 m
checkQuestions.explanation: 148 days is two half-lives (74 + 74), so 80 Ci decays to 40 and then to 20 Ci; 20 Ci × 0.5 R/h per Ci ≈ 10 R/h at 1 m. Planning starts from today's activity, not the certificate's.

checkQuestions.question: The meter reads 32 mR/h at 10 ft from the exposure point. Pending confirmation by survey, the 2 mR/h boundary should fall near:
options: 20 ft
40 ft
80 ft
160 ft
checkQuestions.explanation: 32 ÷ 2 = 16 = 4², so the distance must increase by a factor of 4: about 40 ft. Then walk the line with the meter, because scatter moves calculated boundaries.

checkQuestions.question: Your crank position sits in 480 mR/h open-beam. You install a collimator (factor of 10 in its shielded directions) and double your distance. The new rate is about:
options: 48 mR/h
24 mR/h
120 mR/h
12 mR/h
checkQuestions.explanation: Stacked levers multiply: the collimator gives ÷10 and doubling distance gives ÷4, so 480 ÷ 40 = 12 mR/h.

checkQuestions.question: A crew finishes every quarter under its dose limits, but routinely shoots open-beam because rigging the collimator 'wastes ten minutes.' Against the ALARA concept this practice is:
options: Acceptable - limits are the legal standard
Acceptable if the exposures are short
Unacceptable - dose that is reasonably avoidable is being accepted for convenience
Unacceptable only if a member of the public is nearby
checkQuestions.explanation: ALARA treats limits as ceilings, not allowances. A ten-minute rig that cuts dose rates by a factor of ten in most directions is the definition of reasonably achievable dose reduction.

checkQuestions.question: During exposures in a working yard, the exposure of unbadged plant personnel is controlled primarily by:
options: Issuing them pocket dosimeters for the day
A boundary set by survey near 2 mR/h, posted, and kept under continuous surveillance
Limiting each exposure to under five minutes
Briefing them on radiation hazards at the morning meeting
checkQuestions.explanation: People who are not radiation workers are protected by the measured, watched line - typical regulations hold the public to about 2 mrem in any one hour, and surveillance is what makes the posting real.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course §5.1–5.3
Typical regulations: 10 CFR 20 (dose limits, ALARA) and 10 CFR 34 (industrial radiography operations)
General radiation protection practice: inverse square law, collimation factors, and decay planning values for Ir-192
